mainEntity.name: What's the difference between recording a place and making a photograph?

acceptedAnswer.text: A recording answers 'what was here.' A photograph answers 'what did it feel like to be here, and why did that matter.' Recording is automatic; making a photograph requires deliberate decisions about light, subject, composition, atmosphere, and timing.

mainEntity.name: Why is observation given more weight than shooting?

acceptedAnswer.text: Because seeing is where intention begins. Modern cameras have made the technical phase close to automatic. The differentiator is decisions made before the shutter — where to stand, what to exclude, what light to wait for. Photographers who spend 30 minutes seeing and 10 minutes shooting outperform those who reverse it.

mainEntity.name: Is the rule of thirds a rule or a guideline?

acceptedAnswer.text: A useful starting point, not a destination. Strong photographers ask 'does this frame feel balanced?' rather than 'does it follow the rule?' Balance is felt judgement; the rule of thirds is one approximation of it. Once you can feel balance directly, you'll break the rule for good reasons.

mainEntity.name: How do I develop a photographic style?

acceptedAnswer.text: Through repetition, not preset packs. Style is the accumulation of consistent decisions over years — same kinds of subjects, same compositional habits, same restraint in post. Stop trying to develop a style. Photograph what you're drawn to, consistently. Style emerges from the work.

mainEntity.name: How long should I spend at one location?

acceptedAnswer.text: Longer than you think. Most strong landscape work comes from sessions of an hour or more at a single location. The keeper is rarely the first frame — it's usually the result of 15-20 minutes of observing before committing. Some of my best images came from 5 frames over 30 minutes.

mainEntity.name: Should I edit my photographs heavily or lightly?

acceptedAnswer.text: Lightly, with restraint. Heavy processing usually means the original frame wasn't strong enough. Strong post-processing is mostly invisible — tonal balance, subtle dodging and burning, careful colour control. Photographs that age well are almost always those with the lightest, most considered processing.

mainEntity.name: Will buying a better camera improve my photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: Almost certainly not, after a basic technical floor. The constraint isn't the camera; it's the decisions in front of it. Photographers who improve fastest stop reading gear reviews and shoot more with what they own. Spend the gear-upgrade budget on travel, time, mentoring, or printing instead.

mainEntity.name: How do I know when an image is ready or finished?

acceptedAnswer.text: Two tests. Can you state in one sentence what the photograph is about? Leave the edit for a week and return with fresh eyes. Frames that still feel right after a week are ready; frames that suddenly look over-processed are telling you what you couldn't see when too close to them.
